steps.prompt: Which word is the main clause verb?

hints.Iam: 'Iam' is an adverb meaning 'now / soon / already'
hints.vehementius: 'vehementius' is a comparative adverb meaning 'more vigorously'
hints.vigilare: 'vigilare' is an infinitive in indirect statement

hints.Present indicative: Present would be 'intellegis' — 'intelleges' with -es ending is future
hints.Present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'intellegas' — 'intelleges' is future indicative

hints.lego, legere, legi, lectum: These are the parts of 'legere' (to read/gather) — the base verb
hints.eligo, eligere, elegi, electum: These are the parts of 'eligere' (to choose)
hints.colligo, colligere, collegi, collectum: These are the parts of 'colligere' (to gather)

hints.Ut + subjunctive: 'intellegere' as a verb of mental perception takes acc. + inf., not 'ut'
hints.Quod + indicative: 'quod' clauses are less common after 'intellegere' — acc. + inf. is standard
hints.Ne + subjunctive: 'ne' would introduce a negative purpose — 'intellegere' takes indirect statement

hints.Positive adverb: Positive would be 'vehementer' — 'vehementius' with -ius ending is comparative
hints.Comparative adjective: Adjective would need to agree with a noun — 'vehementius' modifies 'vigilare' and is an adverb

steps.prompt: What does 'multo' do in this sentence?
steps.target: Ablative of degree of difference — 'by much / far more'
choices: Ablative of degree of difference — 'by much / far more'
It is an adverb of time

hints.It means 'many': 'multo' is ablative neuter, not nominative — it modifies the comparative 'vehementius'
hints.It modifies 'me': 'multo' modifies the comparative adverb, not 'me' — it means 'by much'
hints.It is an adverb of time: 'multo' here is ablative of degree of difference used with comparatives

hints.to fight: 'to fight' is 'pugnare' — 'vigilare' means 'to be watchful/keep vigil'

translation: Soon you will understand that I keep watch far more vigorously.
